EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed off the Delmarva peninsula on Monday, January 26th,  and intensified rapidly as it moved slowly northward through the 27th.  Snow spread northward and eastward across the State Monday night and became heavy on Tuesday, the 27th.   Winds became strong during the day Tuesday leading to blizzard conditions at times along and inland from the coast.  The snow persisted into Tuesday night in many areas of western Maine. ||The storm surge associated with the storm, combined with high seas, caused coastal flooding, erosion, and splash over along the southwest coast of Maine.   Portland���s tide gauge peaked at 12.32 feet at 4:18 am on the 27th, about 4 inches above flood levels (see graph below).||Across much of western Maine, snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to more than 30 inches with the highest amounts along and inland from the coast.  The northern mountains generally saw snowfall in the 6 to 10 inch range.  Below are some of the higher 2-day snowfall amounts (in inches) for western Maine Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Once blizzard conditions subsided, snow and blowing snow continued into the early morning of the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developing off the North Carolina coast on the morning of the 3rd moved north to the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 4th, then into the Canadian Maritimes by the morning of the 5th.  The storm brought a moderate to heavy snowfall across Western Maine.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 3 to 6 inches along the coast and interior...to 4 to 8 inches across the foothills and mountains.

Arctic air and gusty winds produced dangerous wind chills across northern Maine from the evening of the 7th through the morning of the 8th. Temperatures of 15 to 25 below zero combined with winds of 10 to 20 mph to produce wind chills of 35 to 45 below zero.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind chill temperatures ranged from 35 to 45 below zero.

Low pressure moved to the Mid Atlantic coastline during the early morning hours of January 27th and rapidly intensified into an intense storm as it moved northeast off the New England coast. The center of the storm stalled east of Cape Cod before slowly moving off to the east on the 28th.  Relatively low astronomical tides accompanied this storm but a moderate storm surge combined with building waves to produce flooding, splash-over and erosion. The storm tide peaked above flood stage in Portland (flood stage 12.0 ft), at 12.3 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense ocean storm produced a moderate storm surge and building waves resulting in coastal flooding in Cumberland County. In Portland, minor inundation occurred along the wharf side streets.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved to the Mid Atlantic coastline during the early morning hours of January 27th and rapidly intensified into an intense storm as it moved northeast off the New England coast. The center of the storm stalled east of Cape Cod before slowly moving off to the east on the 28th.  Relatively low astronomical tides accompanied this storm but a moderate storm surge combined with building waves to produce flooding, splash-over and erosion. The storm tide peaked above flood stage in Portland (flood stage 12.0 ft), at 12.3 feet.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense ocean storm produced a moderate storm surge and building waves resulting in coastal flooding in York County. Splash-over and erosion occurred in Kennebunk, Wells and York. In Saco, water covered roads two feet deep in Camp Ellis and deep pockets of sand filled a city street. Biddeford reported minor flooding along Granite Point Road.

Winds became strong during the day Tuesday leading to blizzard conditions at times along and inland from the coast.  The snow persisted into Tuesday night in many areas of western Maine. ||The storm surge associated with the storm, combined with high seas, caused coastal flooding, erosion, and splash over along the southwest coast of Maine.   Portland���s tide gauge peaked at 12.32 feet at 4:18 am on the 27th, about 4 inches above flood levels (see graph below).||Across much of western Maine, snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to more than 30 inches with the highest amounts along and inland from the coast.  The northern mountains generally saw snowfall in the 6 to 10 inch range.  Below are some of the higher 2-day snowfall amounts (in inches) for western Maine Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Once blizzard conditions subsided, snow and blowing snow continued into the early morning of the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and upper level disturbance crossed the region during the afternoon of the 28th. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was toppled in Abbot by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two men were struck by Lightning in Denmark.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men were injured when lightning apparently struck the house they were about to enter.  Firefighters were called to a home on Route 160 after a lightning strike knocked down power lines, which fell onto a car with the car���s owner inside it.  Denmark firefighters managed to rescue the man from his car. As a firefighter helped the man up the stairs to his home to wait for paramedics, a second lightning strike hit an antenna on the metal roof of the house. The firefighter and homeowner suffered indirect lightning hits. Both were treated and released from a local hospital.

A trough of low pressure developed between an area of low pressure moving east from the Great Lakes and a low developing off the coast.  Moderate to heavy snow developed along the trough as it moved east.  Snowfall amounts were generally in the 2 to 5 inch range across western Maine with 6 to 8 inches across portions of York, Cumberland, Knox, and Waldo Counties.

A cold front and strong upper level disturbance helped produce severe thunderstorms across the region during the afternoon of the 1st. The storms developed during the early afternoon and continued through the late afternoon hours. The storms produced large hail and damaging winds. This marked the start of an active stretch of severe weather...with a persistent upper low located northwest of the region. A series of upper level disturbances and associated surface cold fronts/trofs rotating around the upper low crossed the region during the next several days producing severe weather.

Low pressure moving east from the Mississippi Valley on the 1st intensified off the New England coast on the 2nd before moving rapidly northeastward by the morning of the 3rd.  The storm brought heavy snow to much of western Maine.  Generally amounts ranged from 4 to 12 inches with the greatest amounts along the coast.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure dropping southeast from Canada on the morning of the 14th intensified rapidly as it developed into two separate areas of low pressure southeast of Cape Cod. While the two lows brought a moderate to heavy snow across the extreme southwestern corner of the state and near blizzard conditions along the coast of York County, the impact was much less than had been expected from the single low that was forecast to develop.  Snowfall amounts ranged from an inch or two across most of western Maine to 10 to 20 inches along the York County coast.  Knox County saw 1 to 6 inches from the two storms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and strong upper level disturbance helped produce severe thunderstorms across the region during the afternoon of the 1st. The storms developed during the early afternoon and continued through the late afternoon hours. The storms produced large hail and damaging winds. This marked the start of an active stretch of severe weather...with a persistent upper low located northwest of the region. A series of upper level disturbances and associated surface cold fronts/trofs rotating around the upper low crossed the region during the next several days producing severe weather.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled in Sedgwick by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the afternoon of the 3rd in advance of an approaching upper level disturbance and slow moving cold front. The storms developed during the mid afternoon and persisted into the evening. The storms produced strong damaging winds. Some locations across northeast Aroostook county were struck by several severe storms during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and power lines were toppled by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. Nickel size hail also occurred. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure rapidly intensified along the southern New England coast during the night of the 26th. The low occluded across southern New England during the 27th...then slowly tracked across the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia later on the 27th into the 28th. Heavy snow expanded north across northern and eastern Maine through the night of the 26th into the 27th.|Blizzard conditions developed across Downeast areas during the morning of the 27th with heavy snow and strong winds. At the peak of the event...during the morning and afternoon of the 27th...wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph occurred along the Downeast coast with 40 to 50 mph wind gusts across interior Downeast areas. Travel became nearly impossible with whiteout conditions and snow drifts up to around 10 feet deep. Blizzard conditions diminished during the evening of the 27th though lighter snow and blowing snow persisted into the early morning hours of the 28th. Storm total snow accumulations across Downeast areas generally ranged from 10 to 20 inches with local totals approaching 24 inches. Across the remainder of the region...winter storm conditions persisted through the 27th into the early morning hours of the 28th with heavy snow and blowing snow. Wind gusts across the remainder of the region generally ranged from 25 to 35 mph. Storm total snow accumulations across the remainder of the region generally ranged from 10 to 20 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 16 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure intensified across the Gulf of Maine during the 30th then slowly moved to the maritimes during the 31st. Low pressure rapidly intensified along the southern New England coast during the night of the 26th. The low occluded across southern New England during the 27th...then slowly tracked across the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia later on the 27th into the 28th. Heavy snow expanded north across northern and eastern Maine through the night of the 26th into the 27th.|Blizzard conditions developed across Downeast areas during the morning of the 27th with heavy snow and strong winds. Blizzard conditions diminished during the evening of the 27th though lighter snow and blowing snow persisted into the early morning hours of the 28th. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure developed off the Delmarva peninsula on Monday, January 26th,  and intensified rapidly as it moved slowly northward through the 27th.  Snow spread northward and eastward across the State Monday night and became heavy on Tuesday, the 27th.   Winds became strong during the day Tuesday leading to blizzard conditions at times along and inland from the coast.  The snow persisted into Tuesday night in many areas of western Maine. ||The storm surge associated with the storm, combined with high seas, caused coastal flooding, erosion, and splash over along the southwest coast of Maine.   Portland���s tide gauge peaked at 12.32 feet at 4:18 am on the 27th, about 4 inches above flood levels (see graph below).||Across much of western Maine, snowfall amounts ranged from 10 to more than 30 inches with the highest amounts along and inland from the coast.  The northern mountains generally saw snowfall in the 6 to 10 inch range.  Below are some of the higher 2-day snowfall amounts (in inches) for western Maine Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Once blizzard conditions subsided, snow and blowing snow continued into the early morning of the 28th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved to the New Jersey coast on the 14th of February and rapidly intensified as it moved through the Gulf of Maine early on the 15th. Storm force northerly winds over the coastal waters allowed for near shore waves to build to nearly 20 feet off the York County coast. While the tide never reached flood stage in Portland, Maine, large battering waves led to some beach erosion and splash-over in the York County town of Saco .
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An intense ocean storm produced storm force northerly winds resulting in 20 foot waves off the York County coast. While the tide never reached flood stage in Portland, Maine, large battering waves led to some beach erosion and splash-over in the York County town of Saco.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of low pressure areas moving along a stalled frontal boundary brought a moderate to heavy snowfall to the region over about a 48-hour period.  Snowfall amounts generally ranged from 4 to 8 inches with 6 to 14 inches in coastal York County.

Low pressure developed across the Gulf of Maine during the 17th. The low then intensified while tracking east across the maritimes later on the 17th into the 18th. The low wrapped heavy snow back across eastern Maine. Snow developed during the afternoon and evening of the 17th then persisted into the early afternoon of the 18th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the morning of the 18th. Occasional wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph caused extensive blowing and drifting snow. Even though snow ended during the early afternoon of the 18th...gusty winds produced blowing and drifting snow into the evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 3 to 6 inches. Occasional wind gusts of 35 to 45 mph produced extensive blowing and drifting snow with whiteout conditions at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked from the mid Atlantic region to southern Nova Scotia during the 2nd...then exited across the maritimes through the overnight hours of the 2nd into the morning of the 3rd. Snow developed through the morning of the 2nd then persisted into the early morning hours of the 3rd. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the evening of the 2nd. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches across east-central areas with 10 to 18 inches Downeast. Localized totals of up to around 20 inches also occurred Downeast. Sustained winds of 15 to 25 mph...with occasional gusts of 30 to 40 mph...produced considerable blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 12 to 18 inches...with localized totals up to 22 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked east across the region during the overnight hours of the 4th. The low then intensified across the maritimes during the 5th with a trof extending back across Downeast Maine. Snow developed during the evening of the 4th then persisted through the overnight hours into the afternoon of the 5th. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the morning of the 5th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with localized totals to around 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 10 inches...with localized totals to around 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Snow developed across Downeast Maine during the overnight hours of the 24th in advance of low pressure lifting along the coast. A secondary low then intensified in the Gulf of Maine during the morning of the 25th with a trof extending back across Downeast Maine. The intensifying low and trof helped focus an area of heavy snow which moved east across Downeast Maine through the morning. Snowfall rates of 2 to 4 inches per hour occurred within the area of heavy snow with visibilities reduced to near zero at times. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the morning of the 25th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 5 to 13 inches. The heavy snow...reduced visibilities and slippery road conditions contributed to a multi-car accident involving around 100 vehicles on I-95 just west of Bangor. Around 40 injuries occurred...all non-life threatening. The accident occurred around 730 AM during the morning rush hour. This was the largest multi-vehicle accident ever in Maine. This was the final major event in an active stretch of winter storms which established all time record 30 day snow totals across portions of Downeast Maine...particularly across portions of Washington county.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 6 to 11 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An inverted trough extending northward from an area of low pressure off the coast brought moderate to heavy snow to the mid-coast of Maine.  Snowfall reports included 9 inches in Union, 8 inches in Newcastle and Liberty, 7 inches in Camden and 6 inches in Phippsburg.

Southerly flow ahead of an area of low pressure brought a moderate to heavy snowfall across western sections of the state.  In general, most areas received from 2 to 7 inches of snow with the greatest amounts falling in Knox and Waldo Counties.

An intensifying low pressure system passed well to the west of New England and into Canada during a period of extremely high astronomical tides. Ahead of this system, gale force southerly winds shifted to the southwest and diminished prior to high tide which led to only minor coastal flooding across southwest coastal Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An astronomically high tide of 11.7 feet combined with a storm surge of 0.4 feet to produce a storm tide of 12.1 feet in Portland (flood stage 12.0 ft). The storm tide and fresh water runoff contributed to flooding along the wharf side streets in Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moving east from the Great Lakes on the morning of the 14th merged with another low moving northeast from the southeastern states to form an intensifying area of low pressure in the Gulf of Maine by the morning of the 15th.  The system brought mixed precipitation to the state with 4 to 13 inches of heavy snow across portions of Franklin and Somerset Counties.

Ahead of this system, gale force southerly winds shifted to the southwest and diminished prior to high tide which led to only minor coastal flooding across southwest coastal Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High astronomical tides combined with a modest storm surge to produce minor coastal flooding in Biddeford. Minor flooding occurred on Granite Point Road with minor splash-over occurring along neighboring beaches in York County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure rapidly intensified on the 20th of April over the Great Lakes as a secondary area of low pressure developed along the New England coastline. Gale force easterly winds over the coastal waters allowed for nearshore  waves to build to nearly 15 feet. High astronomical tides coincided with a storm surge of around a foot to produce coastal flooding and minor to moderate splash-over and beach erosion near the time of high tide. ||The tide was above flood stage for nearly an hour at the Portland tide gage. Minor flooding occurred along the pier in Portland and along Granite Point Road in Biddeford. Flooding also occurred in Bath along lower Washington Street near the Kennebec Tavern. Moderate splash-over occurred at Long Beach in York and along Wells Beach where waves over-topped utility poles causing transformers to repeatedly spark.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Intense low pressure produced gale force winds which combined with high astronomical tides to produce flooding along lower Washington Street near the Kennebec Tavern in Bath.

Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred during the afternoon. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 4 to 8 inches.

Low pressure moved eastward from the central plains on the morning of the 26th to near Lake Ontario by the morning of the 27th as a secondary low developed off the New England coast.  While much of the southern and central part of the state saw rain, northern Franklin and central Somerset received heavy snow.  Snowfall amounts ranged from little or no snow across most of southern Maine to 7.8 inches in Eustis, 8.5 inches in Brassua Dam, 9 inches in Jackman, and 10 inches at Pittston Farm.

Intensifying low pressure tracked from the mid Atlantic region to southern Nova Scotia during the 2nd...then exited across the maritimes through the overnight hours of the 2nd into the morning of the 3rd. Snow developed through the morning of the 2nd then persisted into the early morning hours of the 3rd. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the evening of the 2nd. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches across east-central areas with 10 to 18 inches Downeast. Localized totals of up to around 20 inches also occurred Downeast. Sustained winds of 15 to 25 mph...with occasional gusts of 30 to 40 mph...produced considerable blowing and drifting snow.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 10 to 16 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Explosive cyclogenesis occurred along the New England Coast through the night of the 14th into the morning of the 15th. The intense low then slowly tracked across the Gulf of Maine to Nova Scotia through the evening of the 15th. Snow developed across Downeast areas during the afternoon of the 14th. Winds increased through the overnight hours of the 14th into the morning of the 15th. The strongest winds occurred during the morning of the 15th with sustained speeds of 25 to 35 mph and frequent gusts of 40 to 50 mph. Gusts to around 55 mph occurred across coastal Washington county during the morning of the 15th. |Blizzard conditions developed during the morning of the 15th...then persisted into the evening across Washington county...with extensive blowing and drifting snow along with near zero visibilities in whiteouts. Blizzard conditions persisted into early afternoon across coastal Hancock county. Roads became nearly impassible at times within the area of blizzard conditions.  Blizzard warnings were transitioned to Winter Weather Advisories for snow and blowing snow during the early afternoon of the 15th across coastal Hancock county and during the late evening across Washington county. Lighter snow and blowing snow then persisted across Washington county into the 16th. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 10 to 20 inches...with localized totals to around 24 inches...across central and coastal portions of Washington county where the heaviest snows were concentrated. Significantly lesser snow totals occurred across coastal Hancock county with 4 to 7 inches and northern Washington county with 5 to 10 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 10 to 20 inches...with localized totals up to 24 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed on the Aroostook River between Caribou and Fort Fairfield during the late afternoon of the 15th causing flooding. The ice jam flooded portions of Grimes Mill Road...between Caribou and Fort Fairfield...causing the road to be closed. The ice jam slowly moved downriver through the 16th and 17th into the morning of the 18th. Flooding closed Riverside Avenue...in Fort Fairfield...during the evening of the 16th. The bridge crossing the Aroostook River near Fort Fairfield was also closed on several occasions due to elevated river levels. The ice jam then moved east of the region during the morning of the 18th allowing river levels to begin falling.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River between Caribou and Fort Fairfield caused flooding. The flooding closed portions of the Grimes Mill Road and Riverside Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed on the Saint John River...near Dickey...during the early morning hours of the 16th causing flooding. The river crested around a half foot above flood stage. The flooding caused a closure of the Big Rapids Road for several hours. The ice jam released and moved downriver later in the morning allowing river levels to fall. Another ice jam during the morning of the 17th caused the river to hover around flood stage into the early afternoon before also releasing and allowing river levels to drop.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Saint John River near Dickey closed portions of Big Rapids Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed on the Saint John River...near Saint Francis...during the morning of the 18th. River levels rose through the 18th...with flooding developing that evening. Route 161 was closed  at the Allagash and Saint Francis town line during the evening of the 18th due to flooding. A truck stalled while attempting to drive through the flood waters and had to be pulled from the flood waters. The ice jam persisted into the early morning hours of the 19th before releasing and allowing river levels to fall. Route 161 was re-opened during the early morning hours of the 19th once river levels fell and ice was removed from the road.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Saint John River near Saint Francis caused the closure of a portion of Route 161 at the Saint Francis and Allagash town line.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An ice jam developed on the Aroostook River...near Washburn...during the evening of the 17th causing flooding. The river crested at 21.1 feet later that evening...around 7 feet above flood stage. This established a new record crest for the site which had previously been 20.9 feet. Flooding closed portions of Gardner Creek Road and Parsons Road with water depths occasionally exceeding 2 feet. Two homes along Gardner Creek Road were also impacted by the flooding. One of the homes had a flooded basement. The second home was inundated with 5 feet of water in the basement. The ice jam persisted into the evening of the 19th. Though river levels fluctuated...flooding persisted into the evening of the 19th. The ice jam released during the evening of the 19th allowing river levels to fall.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An ice jam on the Aroostook River at Washburn caused flooding which closed portions of Gardner Creek Road and Parsons Road. The river reached an all time record crest of 21.1 feet...exceeding the previous record of 20.9 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trof and upper level disturbance crossed the region during the afternoon and evening of the 27th.  The trof and upper level disturbance combined with unseasonably warm humid conditions to produce severe thunderstorms across northern Maine. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and persisted into the evening. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled on power lines in Madawaska by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A pre-frontal trof and upper level disturbance crossed the region during the afternoon and evening of the 27th.  The trof and upper level disturbance combined with unseasonably warm humid conditions to produce severe thunderstorms across northern Maine. Thunderstorms developed during the afternoon and persisted into the evening. The thunderstorms produced damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were toppled in Saint Agatha and along Long Lake by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph. A house under construction was also damaged on Pelletier Island in Long Lake. The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Intensifying low pressure tracked from the mid Atlantic region to southern Nova Scotia during the 2nd...then exited across the maritimes through the overnight hours of the 2nd into the morning of the 3rd. Snow developed through the morning of the 2nd then persisted into the early morning hours of the 3rd. Warning criteria snow accumulations occurred through the evening of the 2nd. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 12 inches across east-central areas with 10 to 18 inches Downeast. Localized totals of up to around 20 inches also occurred Downeast. Sustained winds of 15 to 25 mph...with occasional gusts of 30 to 40 mph...produced considerable blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep tropical moisture was drawn north across the region in advance of a slow moving cold front while low pressure lifted north along the front. In addition to the tropical moisture...a strong low level jet provided additional Atlantic moisture. The moisture was focused along the front and low with additional strong upper level support. The result was very heavy rain. Heavy rain developed during the morning of September 30th then persisted through that afternoon. Rainfall totals of 4.00 to 6.00 inches were common across southern Piscataquis county...with localized totals around 7.00 inches. The mainstem of the Piscataquis River crested slightly above flood stage during the early morning hours of the 1st with no significant impacts. The river began to rise above flood stage during the late evening of September 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mainstem of the Piscataquis River at Dover-Foxcroft briefly crested slightly above flood stage during the early morning hours of the 1st.  No significant impacts were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong low pressure tracked northwest of Maine from the night of the 28th through the 29th. The low drew a cold front across the region during the afternoon of the 29th. A strong low level jet crossed the region in advance of the front through the morning hours of the 29th. Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts of around 55 to 65 mph...occurred along coastal portions of Washington and Hancock counties. A peak wind gust of 66 mph was reported near Lubec in coastal Washington county. The strong winds toppled trees and power lines. Power outages peaked at around 8500 customers during the early morning hours of the 29th mostly across Hancock county. A line of low topped convection formed along the front during the late afternoon of the 29th which also produced locally damaging thunderstorm winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Sustained winds of 25 to 35 mph...with gusts up to around 55 to 65 mph...occurred during the morning of the 29th. Measured wind gusts of 66 mph were reported near Lubec with 61 mph at Eastport.  The winds toppled trees and brought down power lines contributing to power outages.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture was drawn north across the region in advance of a slow moving cold front. An area of heavy rain...with embedded thunderstorms...developed in advance of the front during the early morning hours of the 26th. Areas of heavy rain and thunderstorms trained across coastal Washington county through the early morning hours of the 26th producing 3.00 to 5.00 inches of rain. Flash flooding developed across the Machias...Marshfield and Whitneyville areas and persisted through late morning.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An area of heavy rain...with embedded thunderstorms...trained across coastal Washington county through the early morning hours of the 26th. Rainfall totals of 3.00 to 5.00 inches occurred during the early morning hours. These rains led to flash flooding in the Machias...Marshfield and Whitneyville areas during the morning of the 26th. Street flooding was reported in Machias with water depths of 2 to 3 feet on roads and road closures. Numerous vehicles were stalled due to the flooding. Some structures were also flooded or surrounded by flood waters. In Marshfield...a portion of Route 192 was washed out due to flash flooding. In Whitneyville...numerous small shoulder washouts were reported along South Main Street.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture was drawn north across the region in advance of a slow moving cold front. Training showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain developed in advance of the front during the evening of the 21st and persisted across southern portions of Piscataquis county through the evening. Rainfall totals of up to around 5.00 inches occurred across portions of southern Piscataquis county through the evening hours of the 21st. Localized flash flooding developed in the Guilford and Parkman areas during the late evening of the 21st and persisted into the early morning hours of the 22nd. Small streams and brooks overflowed their banks damaging roads and causing road closures.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training showers and thunderstorms with very heavy rain produced localized rainfall totals of up to around 5.00 inches across portions of southern Piscataquis county during the evening of the 21st. These rains produced flash flooding in the Guilford and Parkman areas from the late evening of the 21st into the early morning hours of the 22nd. Several streets in the town of Guilford were closed due to flash flooding. Crow Hill Road and Bridge Road around Parkman received significant damage due to washouts from flash flooding.  Several small streams and brooks in the Guilford and Parkman areas overflowed their banks producing flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong shortwave swinging in from the west rapidly increased shear across the region ahead of an approaching cold front on the afternoon of June 23rd. Numerous strong to severe cells developed across southwest Maine with wind damage being the main feature of this event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees and wires on Route 1, Foster Avenue and Frost Brook Road in Freeport.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front and strong upper level disturbance helped produce severe thunderstorms across the region during the afternoon of the 1st. The storms developed during the early afternoon and continued through the late afternoon hours. The storms produced large hail and damaging winds. This marked the start of an active stretch of severe weather...with a persistent upper low located northwest of the region. A series of upper level disturbances and associated surface cold fronts/trofs rotating around the upper low crossed the region during the next several days producing severe weather.

Severe thunderstorms developed across northern Maine during the afternoon of the 3rd in advance of an approaching upper level disturbance and slow moving cold front. The storms developed during the mid afternoon and persisted into the evening. The storms produced strong damaging winds. Some locations across northeast Aroostook county were struck by several severe storms during the event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The roof was blown off a potato house by wind gusts estimated at 60 mph. The time is estimated.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Utility poles were snapped along Route 9...with trees and wires toppled across the rest of town...by wind gusts estimated at 65 mph.

Severe thunderstorms developed along the front during the early afternoon and continued through the late afternoon hours. The storms produced large hail and strong damaging winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The time is estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Almost 6 inches of rain fell at the Portland Jetport in a 4-hour period on the morning of September 30th with 2.22 inches falling in one hour between 11 AM and noon. This caused streets to quickly fill with water and many roads were closed due to flash flooding. Numerous cars were abandoned and some businesses were flooded. A daycare had to be evacuated due to flood waters entering the building.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Almost 6 inches of rain fell in a 4-hour period at the Portland Jetport with 2.22 inches falling in one hour. Flash flooding occurred along Marginal Way, St. John Street, Park Ave., Marston Street , Congress Ave., and Coyle Street. Many cars were abandoned and roads closed due to the flooding. Some businesses were flooded and a daycare had to be evacuated because of water entering the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure tracked northeast along a stalled frontal boundary along the coast. Gale force southeasterly winds combined with the highest astronomical tides of the month to produce coastal flooding across the southwest coast of Maine. Nearshore waves built to eight feet off the coast during high tide and led to splash-over in York County. The storm surge values were minimal, however tropical moisture triggered extreme rainfall and runoff in local estuaries. The high tide at Portland was 12.13 ft.  The high tide combined with heavy rain from this storm, the majority of which fell within a few hours of high tide, contributed to the freshwater flash flooding as the runoff had no where to go. There was coastal flooding along Commercial Street where cars were flooded. Farther south, Granite Point Road in Biddeford was flooded at high tide with several inches of water. In Kennebunk, many roads were closed due to heavy splash-over. Minor splash-over occurred in Wells, while low lying areas flooded along the Long Beach Avenue area of York.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain combined with gale force winds from an ocean storm and high astronomical tides to produce coastal flooding along Commercial Street in Portland.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure moved east across the region during the 27th. Snow developed during the early morning hours of the 27th...persisted through mid afternoon...then ended during the late afternoon and early evening.  Warning criteria snow accumulations were met during the late afternoon. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 6 to 8 inches...with localized totals up to 10 inches in the Madawaska and Frenchville area of the Saint John Valley in northern Aroostook county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Overrunning snow developed during the early morning hours of the 29th between low pressure approaching from the west and retreating high pressure. A secondary low then developed across the Gulf of Maine during the 29th while the approaching low weakened. Snow persisted through the afternoon into the evening...then ended during the late evening with the low exiting across the maritimes. Warning criteria snow accumulations were met through the late afternoon and evening hours. Storm total snow accumulations generally ranged from 7 to 10 inches...with localized totals up to around 12 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 7 to 12 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure developed along the Maine coast during the early morning hours of the 3rd. The low then intensified while tracking east across Nova Scotia through the 3rd. Warning criteria snow accumulations were met during the evening. Snow then ended during the late evening hours with the low exiting across the maritimes. Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm total snow accumulations ranged from 5 to 8 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak and stalled frontal boundary across northern New Hampshire through central Maine was the focus for afternoon convection.  These storms produced large hail and damaging winds in many locations across southern and central Maine.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind damage caused by a microburst snapped and uprooted a couple hundred trees along Bog Road in Albion just north of the intersection with Route 9.  The winds also tore metal roofing materials off several buildings, and blew over and demolished a camper to house chickens.   About 80 chickens perished in the storm.  The microburst was accompanied by wind-driven hail up to 1 inch in diameter which caused an estimated $50,000 in damages to crops in the area and about $25,000 in damage to buildings and livestock .

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front approaching from the west initiated afternoon convection across Eastern New York and Western New England on the afternoon of the 19th. High instability and increasing shear helped to develop more organized supercells and lines of thunderstorms.  Large hail and damaging winds were prevalent with these storms.

High instability and increasing shear helped to develop more organized supercells and lines of thunderstorms.  Large hail and damaging winds were prevalent with these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees at Douglas and Hathaway Roads in Brownfield.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Low pressure in the Gulf States  on the morning of the 28th moved northeast to the Great Lake by the morning of the 29th as a secondary low developed off the mid-Atlantic coast.  The secondary low moved rapidly northeast during the day on the 29th.  The combination of the two systems brought moderate to heavy snow across the State.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 inches to 10 inches across the region.

A strong shortwave and associated cold front moved into northwestern New England on the afternoon of May 28th. A warm and humid airmass was in place across the region ahead of the front. Convection began firing during the early afternoon in western New England and quickly moved into New Hampshire. Damaging winds were the main feature of these storms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm downed trees in Palmyra.
